narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 32, and strong showings in your school's Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles—both placing you in the top decile among your peers. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 68% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark this university uses to evaluate future success in legal practice. This high risk of failure led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and thrive in their program. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 academic decile, which stood out as a major factor pushing the prediction toward failure despite your top ranking, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, which similarly contributed strongly to this concerning outcome. In contrast, your family income level worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward bar exam success, much like your LSAT and undergraduate GPA did to a lesser degree—though these positive influences weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Your choice to pursue full-time studies had a negligible impact either way. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where average Year 1 and Year 3 deciles hover around the middle of the pack (5th or 6th), your elite rankings should have been strengths, but the model interpreted them in this case as heightening the failure risk—perhaps signaling other underlying patterns it detected. Your LSAT and GPA were below the dataset averages of 36.7 and 3.21, respectively, which tempered but didn't drive the decision. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given your personal achievements as a white male from a supportive upper-middle-class home, it doesn't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the average, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your Year 1 and 3 successes in context. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift the model's view in your favor, and many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting schools with more holistic reviews. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing.
